step_by_step_thinking:
I agree with option D—papilledema—is the only condition among these listed that physically enlarges the optic disc through swelling, thereby expanding the anatomical boundary of the blind spot visible during ophthalmoscopic evaluation. Glaucoma primarily results in neuroretinal damage and peripheral vision loss, not blind spot enlargement; diabetic macular edema impacts central vision centrally; and optic nerve hypoplasia reduces disc size. Thus, D remains the most accurate choice based on pathophysiology and clinical observation.

Answer: D

I agree with Option D – Papilledema. One key point supporting this is that swelling of the optic disc increases its diameter, which directly expands the area corresponding to the blind spot on funduscopic imaging, unlike other choices that affect different parts of the retina or involve degeneration instead of structural growth.


Answer: D
